14.1 Limitations of Current Materials Planning and
Replenishment Tools
Let’s first evaluate why traditional materials planning tools are falling short of address-
ing the challenges of the VUCA world. It’s important for you to understand that while
many of these planning tools effectively work in isolation, they can’t work cohesively
enough to reduce or eliminate the bullwhip effect. And as we evaluate each material
planning tool to explain why it’s falling short, it’s equally important for you to under-
stand that implementing a DDR solution doesn’t entail discarding all these planning
tools, such as forecasting, MRP, safety stock, and reorder point planning. Rather, it
requires you to use selected and effective features of several of these planning tools to
improve the flow of materials and relevant information. For example, DDR still uses
MRP for quantity calculation and scheduling of components, but it stops at stocked or
buffered positions. In contrast, MRP starts at the lowest level of a BOM, which is often
the highest level of the product (e.g., a finished product), and it continues quantity cal-
culation and scheduling of all components of the BOM until it reaches the highest level
of a BOM (often the raw materials and packing materials). Similarly, the forecasting tool
is still a required planning tool in the overall context of using DDR, as is using capacity
requirements planning tools in the context of overall supply chain planning.
Bullwhip Effect
The bullwhip effect is a cascading and incremental increase in the logistics and supply
chain’s variability as a result of a small change in customer demand.
The most important material planning tools and their current limitations are as fol-
lows:
 Forecasting
Forecasting uses historical data to predict or forecast future requirements and uses
forecasting models, such as constant, moving average, seasonal, and trend. All fore-
casts also have a degree of error to account for deviations (mean average deviations)
between forecast figures and the confidence in these figures. However, forecasts
start out wrong, and they are riddled with errors that only increase with time. Fore-
casts are also more inaccurate when they are made at the individual stock-keeping
unit level. Imagine trying to accurately forecast or predict the number of air condi-
tioners of a specific model (individual stock-keeping units) to produce a year from
© 

771
14.1 Limitations of Current Materials Planning and Replenishment Tools
now (over a long-term horizon) when there just isn’t enough information available
now and predicting how hot the weather is going to be a year from now is unreliable.
 Sales and operations planning (S&OP)
Forecasting often forms the basis of S&OP. A sales and operations plan ensures that
the annual sales plan matches up with the production (operations) plan. The mutually
agreed-upon sales and operations plan then enables a company to evaluate the ware-
house space, the machines’ capacities, and the procurement commitment (value) it
will need for the entire year. However, when inaccurate forecasting forms the basis of
S&OP, sales and production plans are most certainly bound to change over the near-
term horizon when actual sales and demand are better known.
 Demand management
Demand management is the last step for a materials planner to check and validate a
sales or production plan to use for production and procurement planning by using
the master production scheduling and MRP tools. (The plan is driven by the S&OP of
the previous step.) The basis of demand management is an unreliable forecast,
which leads to precisely wrong procurement and proposals generation.
 Master production scheduling (MPS)
An MPS program plans and schedules production of critical materials and ensures
that there are no changes to the production plan within the defined time horizon,
such as within a week. No changes are made to the master production scheduling
after an MRP run. MPS runs before MRP so that critical products and components are
planned first in the planning process. As the demand management plan is fed into
MPS, it’s clear to see why MPS only adds to the supply chain chaos.
 Material requirements planning (MRP)
MRP explodes the BOMs of finished products to calculate the quantities of the semi-
finished, packing, and raw materials required to produce finished products.
 Materials planning using safety stock
Safety stock is the buffer stock of materials that planners turn to in case of a sudden
and unforeseen demand spike or a supply disruption. Although it’s possible to auto-
matically calculate safety stock, safety stock is unable to account for the dynamic
nature of supply and demand variabilities. Furthermore, the safety stock calculation
method isn’t the primary materials planning tool and therefore cannot truly act as a
buffer between demand and supply deviations.
 Reorder point planning
When stock falls below a reorder point, replenishment is triggered. The replenish-
ment’s proposal quantity is often a fixed or minimum lot that’s intended to prevent
the ERP system from creating an unnecessarily large number of procurement pro-
posals of small quantities each time there’s a material shortage (or when a material’s
stock falls below the reorder point). As with safety stock, you can calculate the reorder
point of a material automatically; however, reorder point planning can’t reflect the
replenishment priorities of different materials.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
772

sections.title: Material and Information Flows
sections.text:
14.2 Material and Information Flows
According to George W. Plossl, author of Production and Inventory Control: Principles
and Techniques (Prentice Hall, 1967), “All benefits will be directly related to the speed of
flow of information and materials.” Now more than ever, we can enhance this concept
by adding that the flow of materials and information must be relevant to the required
out-of-market information because in today’s hyperconnected world, companies are
drowning in data but starving for relevant information to help them navigate the
VUCA challenges of the modern supply chain. Furthermore, the words all benefits can
be broken down into the following benefits or KPIs that companies seek to achieve:
 Exceptional customer service ensures timely and quality products.
 Revenue increases or at least remains stable.
 The quality of products and services keeps getting better.
 Inventories of purchased, WIP, and produced products stay at optimal levels.
 Expenses remain minimal by eliminating expedited freight costs, rework, and
unplanned partial deliveries.
 Cash flow is greatly improved.
With these points in mind, it’s critically important for you to address distortions lead-
ing to relevant information or material flows as often and as soon as possible to create
a smoother supply chain.
First, let’s look at why distortions of relevant material and information occur, as illus-
trated in Figure 14.1. Starting at the bottom of Figure 14.1, materials planners use MRP 1
to calculate future demand for products to ensure timely production and procure-
ment. However, due to its inherent logic, MRP treats everything as dependent and acts
as one big calculator to suggest production and procurement quantities of materials on
specific dates. In today’s complex and globalized supply chain, the time it takes to pro-
cure and produce a product is much longer than it has ever been before. At the same
time, customers today have far more options to choose from, and that leads to shrink-
ing customer tolerance time for a company to deliver or ensure availability of a prod-
uct 2.
To better predict customer demand and feed that data into the MRP program, compa-
nies use forecasting tools 3. Unfortunately, even the best forecasting model or pro-
gram always has a degree of error. The longer the forecast horizon is—such as yearly,
quarterly, or even monthly—the greater the degree of error 4. The more detailed the
level at which forecasting is done, such as at the individual product or stock keeping
unit level, the more amplified the forecasting error, which leads to the distortion of rel-
evant information 8.
Refer again to the bottom of Figure 14.1. While MRP uses forecasting to predict demand
3, when the actual customers’ demand is known, adjustment between forecasted
demand and actual demand must happen 5, and that leads to nervousness 6 in the
© 

773
14.2 Material and Information Flows
logistics and supply chain. These conflicting and often-changing demand signals 7
again lead to the distortion of relevant information 8. Using distorted or unreliable
information for materials planning is one of the two major reasons for the bullwhip
effect, which is the amplification of supply variability as the result of demand variability
and which has cascading and negative ripple effects on the supply chain.
The second effect of nervousness 6 is that companies often use materials planning,
such as production or procurement planning with time horizons of one-week or even
two-week buckets 9, to bring some sanity to production or procurement plans and to
prevent frequent changes to these plans. In doing so, planners use latent signals j that
again result in the distortion of relevant information 8.
The third factor that adds nervousness 6 to the supply chain appears when materials
planners attempt to flatten BOMs k to reduce material quantities or scheduling vari-
abilities. In doing so, materials planners end up using oversimplified signals l for pro-
curement or production planning, which further adds to the distortion of relevant
information 8. When distorted information is used, it’s no surprise that the products
or materials required aren’t ready or available when needed m, and that eventually cul-
minates in the distortion of relevant materials n. When relevant materials are dis-
torted, companies either end up with products that don’t sell (which is an overstock
scenario) or with no products to deliver on time to meet demand (which is an out-of-
stock scenario); both scenarios lead to missed business opportunities and lost custom-
ers.
Let’s now go back again to the bottom of Figure 14.1 to understand how the MRP pro-
gram that treats all materials as dependent 1 leads to the same distortions of relevant
materials n created by nervousness 6 in the supply chain previously. Just like a big cal-
culator, the MRP program performs a level-by-level explosion of BOMs of finished
goods to calculate the quantities of semifinished, packing, and raw materials that are
required at specific times; the resultant MRP plans of quantities and schedules are
based on all these dependencies o. Because the MRP program is designed to ensure
that the quantities of all materials in a BOM eventually net to zero after taking any
safety stock into account, any change in plans, variability, or disruption on the supply
side has multiplying effects on all dependent requirements, leading to the accumula-
tion of delays p. The delays accumulate, but the gains do not, and thus, material plan-
ners have MRP-generated production and procurement plans that are never properly
synchronized q between demand and supply. The end results are the same as before:
companies end up with overstock of slow-moving products or products that don’t sell,
while stock-outs occur for high-demand products.
In the following sections, we’ll look at how decoupling can serve as a solution to this
problem, and we’ll also cover the basics of demand-driven material requirements plan-
ning (DDMRP) and its components.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
774

Permission)

14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and
Components
The solution to the distortion of relevant information and the quagmire of material
flows lies in decoupling the logistics and supply chains to reduce the bullwhip effect.
Decoupling entails disconnecting the ever-present and constantly increasing variabil-
ity between demand and supply by placing buffers at strategic points, known as decou-
pling points. Materials planners then regularly monitor the buffers in the material
value chain—from procurement to interplant transfer to production to distribution—
to ensure timely action and interventions where necessary. There are three types of
buffers:

14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components
1. Stock buffer
The stock situation at each buffer is closely monitored to ensure that there’s no
shortage of the required materials. The icon denotes a decoupled position and
maintains a stock buffer in DDR.
2. Time buffer
This buffer manages demand and supply variability that may impact production or
procurement scheduling. The time buffer icons are these:
3. Capacity buffer
This buffer at production lines or work centers provides the cushions required for
smoother flow to in-house-produced materials. The capacity buffer icons are these:
The three color codes represent different buffer statuses: red points the materials plan-
ners to areas that need immediate attention or urgent action, yellow requires closely
monitoring the buffer situation, and green reflects a comfortable buffer situation. Each
color of buffer is also accompanied by a percentage to denote the buffer remaining or
unconsumed in relation to the overall buffer size. For example, a work center, such as a
tablet-packing line, might show a red color in the tricolor buffer status to indicate 12%
remaining (unconsumed) capacity. All reports and metrics used to represent buffers
show red at the top (or highest) level to reflect the criticality or severity of the buffer sit-
uation, while green gets the lowest position. Exceptional situations such as stock-outs,
overstocks, and overcapacities are denoted by different colors, such as deep red for
stock-outs or overcapacities and deep blue for overstocks or excess available capacities.
As this chapter will discuss later, these deep-red and dark-blue zones are often the best
candidates for bringing improvements to material and information flows.
Note
SAP introduced DDR in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 1708 and SAP S/4HANA 1709. DDR is based
on the DDMRP methodology from the Demand Driven Institute (DDI). Carol Ptak and
Chad Smith, partners at DDI, have committed themselves to promoting and proliferat-
ing DDMRP and associated concepts and working throughout the world. In their book
DDMRP: Demand Driven Materials Requirement Planning (3rd edition, Industrial Press,
2019), Ptak and Smith provide a deep dive into and authoritative resources on DDMRP
and associated concepts.
Figure 14.2 shows an example planning screen for DDR with several parts, with varying
priorities denoted by corresponding tricolor buffer statuses.
Figure 14.3 represents the supply chain. Starting from the left, a buffer is placed at the
decoupling point 1, which is between the component manufacturing and subassem-
bly steps. Notice the reduction in the variability wave 2 that dampens the bullwhip
effect and also notice the associated lead time reduction 3.

tics and supply chain but also the five main components of DDMRP.

14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components
1. Purchasing
Purchased parts often experience supply variability.
2. Operations
Production operations that produce several common parts and work centers with
bottleneck capacities are the prime candidates for DDR. For semifinished goods sold
by a company directly to its customers, implementing DDR can mitigate the risks of
demand variability due to customers’ changing requirements for the company’s
products.
3. Distribution
Finished goods or even services’ spare parts are distributed to different distribution
centers and experience significant demand variability.
4. Interplant
Companies with several manufacturing units or warehouses spread across various
locations of a country, different countries, or even continents have materials with
short, medium, or long lead times. Due to the special procurement types of such
materials, some of them are prime candidates for DDR.

14.3.2 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Components
The five components of DDMRP (see Figure 14.4) show a materials planner how to effec-
tively position buffers at strategic inventory locations, protect the flow of materials and
relevant information by evaluating buffer profiles, and then dynamically adjust these
buffers. Finally, an efficient pull system is put in place to reduce variability between
demand and supply through demand-driven planning.
Figure 14.4 Five Components of DDMRP (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission)
This is followed by execution while ensuring that the changing supply and demand
dynamics adapt to the logistics and supply chain.

Strategic Inventory Positioning
In this component (see Figure 14.4), a materials planner decides which items to stock
and where. Materials that are prime candidates for consideration are those with long
lead times, significant supply or demand variability, or a component used in manufac-
turing several products, plus those that face a production capacity bottleneck. The
planner chooses materials from the stock buffers as well as the decoupling points in
the logistics and supply chain. As already covered previously, the buffers at decoupling
points are denoted by the icon.
The following two questions will immediately arise in your mind when you’re choos-
ing a decoupling point:
 What important factors should I consider while selecting a decoupling point in the
logistics and supply chain?
 Where should I position a decoupling point in the logistics and supply chain?
The six factors you should consider when choosing a decoupling point are as follows:
 Customer tolerance time
The maximum amount of time customers are willing to wait for delivery or avail-
ability of a product before seeking alternatives
 Market potential lead time
Lead time that will allow an increase in price or an opportunity to capture additional
business through existing or new customer channels
 Demand or sales order visibility horizon
The time period during which the demand for dependent requirements is known or
when there’s visibility into actual sale of products as recorded in a sales order
 External variabilities
– Demand variability
The probability of spikes and swings in demand that may overwhelm resources
such as capacity, stock, and cash flow
– Supply variability
The potential for and probability of supply disruptions from specific sources of
supply (suppliers or interplant transfers)
 Inventory leverage and flexibility
The specific places in an integrated BOM structure that enable a company to have
maximum options for product availability and the best lead time compression
 Critical operation protection
Putting specific control points in place to minimize potential points of disruptions
in the supply chain, such as placing control points at work centers or production
resources with bottleneck capacities or where demand/supply variabilities can
quickly accumulate or amplify
© 

779
14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components
Based on these six factors for choosing decoupling points, Figure 14.5 shows an exam-
ple of strategically placed buffers. These buffers are based on the following consider-
ations:
 The FPG and 201 buffers let you quickly take advantage of additional business oppor-
tunities.
 The FPG stock position is minimized due to the 101 buffer.
 The 101 and 102P buffers allow for an MTO strategy on FPH.
 The 201 and 204P buffers protect the Z resource as best as possible at the product
structure (BOM) level.
 The 301P buffer minimizes the inventory liability at 201.
 The 201 and 101 buffers can direct the shared components to actual requirements.
Figure 14.5 Strategically Placed Buffers (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission)
The following are the six tests you should consider when evaluating a decoupling
point:
 Decoupling test
The lead time on the supply side should decrease when compared with the lead time
chain of the nonbuffered components.
 Bidirectional benefit test
The demand side should show a compressed or reduced lead time and higher avail-
ability of a product or a component. The supply side should get cumulative demand
requirements to provide a materials planner with better and more efficient order
management of the recommended order quantity.
 Order independence test
The stock in the buffers isn’t specific to any production order, sales order, or stock
transport order (interplant transfer). This test therefore ensures that there’s as little
WIP as possible while making stock available for any demand.

 Primary planning mechanism test
The buffered part at a decoupling point should be able to manage all demand and
supply requirements independently by taking the net flow position into account.
 Relative priority test
The buffered position needs to account for both planning and execution of not just
the buffered part but also its priority in relation to other buffered parts and posi-
tions.
 Dynamic adjustment test
The buffered part needs to adjust dynamically to account for supply and demand
variations within the defined time horizon.
Buffer Profiles and Levels
In this component (see Figure 14.4), you as a materials planner decide how much stock
to hold (the buffers) at specific decoupling points. You group together materials that
share similar characteristics and attributes (e.g., raw or packing materials with long
lead times, finished goods with high demand variability, several intermediate products
sharing a common machine or a bottlenecked work center) and then assign them rele-
vant buffer profiles. Then, you maintain buffer levels.
Let’s take a look at the three buffer zones (red, yellow, and green) and how to calculate
them (see Figure 14.6). You calculate the yellow zone 1, and then the green zone 2, and
finally the red zone 3.
 Yellow zone
This is central to the demand coverage in the buffer, and you calculate it using the
following formula:
Average daily usage (ADU) × Decoupled lead time (DLT)
 Green zone
This determines the order frequency and the size of each order. There are three ways
to calculate the green zone, and the largest value of the three calculations forms the
green buffer zone:
– Minimum order quantity (MOQ)
– Desired order cycle (DOC) × DLT
– ADU × DLT × Lead time factor (LT factor)
 Red zone
This is the embedded safety in the buffer. The red zone is the sum of the red zone
base and the red zone safety, as follows:
– Red zone base: ADU × DLT × LT factor
– Red zone safety: Red zone base × Variability factor (VF)
– Red zone = Red zone base + Red zone safety
© 

781
14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components
Figure 14.6 Buffer Zones (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission)
To get a better sense of buffer positions, consider the following four abbreviations and
their meanings:
 Top of red (TOR)
This is the highest point of the red zone. When the stock, schedule, or capacity
breaches the red zone, it signals the need for urgent action or immediate attention.
 Top of yellow (TOY)
This is the highest point of the yellow zone. Breaching TOY signals a warning of a
buffer situation. TOY = Red zone + Yellow zone.
 Top of green (TOG)
This is the highest point of the green zone that reflects a comfortable buffer position
until it crosses into the TOY zone. TOG = Red + Yellow + Green.
 Over the top of green (OTOG)
This is a situation in which the on-hand or available stock is over the top of the
defined green zone (the TOG), and it signals an excess inventory position.
Figure 14.7 shows an example of calculating the buffer zones. You start by calculating
the yellow zone, and then you calculate the green zone, and finally the red zone. The
data required for calculating all three zones is also shown in Figure 14.7. The three zones
are calculated as follows:

Dynamic Adjustments
In this component (see Figure 14.4), a materials planner oversees dynamic adjustments
to buffers to account for product seasonality, the stage of a product maturity (such as a
soon-to-be-discontinued product or a new product introduction), and capacity adjust-
ments, and then, the planner prebuilds the requirements in anticipation of future
demand or slowdown. Buffers, such as stock buffers, also adjust dynamically to account
for supply variability, a procurement or production schedule mismatch, or a machine
reaching an overload capacity situation. As already covered, the stock, schedule, and
capacity buffers adjust dynamically to ensure elimination of distortions of relevant
information and materials flows.
Demand-Driven Planning
In this component (see Figure 14.4), a materials planner uses the visual planning tools
to create replenishment orders where necessary. Replenishment orders are created
based on the net flow equation:
Net flow equation: On hand + On order – Qualified sales order (or demand)
In this equation, on hand is the available stock in the warehouse, on order refers to the
quantities of materials ordered and awaiting receipt, and qualified sales order (or
demand) refers to sales orders that are due today, those that are past due, and qualified
future spikes. The qualified future spike considers the quantity threshold within a
defined time horizon in a daily time bucket to decide if the spike qualifies to be included
in net flow equation calculation. For example, a qualified future spike is included in the

Visible and Collaborative Execution
In this component (see Figure 14.8), you as a materials planner take action to convert
suggested replenishment orders into procurement orders, production orders, or stock
transport orders. Furthermore, you use material lead times or scheduling synchroniza-
tion alerts to quickly attend to any material or scheduling disruptions that threaten to
destabilize the supply chain. DDR is a priority-based pull system that alerts planners
visually whenever a situation warrants action or attention.
Figure 14.8 DDMRP Execution (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission)

where to position the decoupling point buffers. Finally, you classify the products

in the SAP S/4HANA system, which we discuss in detail in the following sections.
14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
To begin with, this section will focus on configuration settings to explain how they
impact DDR master data and even business processes. Then, we’ll discuss setting up the
minimal DDR-specific master data to get the DDR process up and running in the system.
Next, we’ll cover some transactions or real-time business processes that DDR takes into
consideration to calculate its own set of parameters. These parameters calculate the
three difference zones (green, yellow, and red) and set up the right classifications (ABC,
EFG, PQR, XYZ, etc.).

As the entire DDR process is embedded in various stages of the logistics and supply
chain—such as procurement, production, distribution, and sales—it’s best that all rele-
vant stakeholders gain a better understanding of an end-to-end DDR process flow.
Let’s discuss configuration settings first.

sections.title: Configuration Settings
sections.text:
14.4.1 Configuration Settings
The configuration settings related to DDR are buffer profile, key terms, MRP type, and
lot sizing, and these are discussed in the following sections.
Buffer Profile
According to the DDMRP principles, products must first be classified according to vari-
ous classifications, such as the usage value (the ABC classification), lead time (the EFG
classification), BOM usage (the PQR classification), and variability (the XYZ classifica-
tion).
Depending on the settings of the variability and the lead time classification made in a
buffer profile, SAP S/4HANA will determine a variability factor (VF) and a lead time fac-
tor (LT factor). Both of these factors are predetermined in a buffer profile and are used
to calculate the buffer. Figure 14.9 shows the standard buffer profile delivered by SAP
S/4HANA, which can be found in the Buffer Profile Maintenance app. Alternatively, the
buffer profile can also be accessed using Transaction PPH_DD_BUF_PROF in the SAP
S/4HANA system.
Figure 14.9 Standard Buffer Profile
Let’s consider an example of how the system determines the VF and the LT factor from
the buffer profile. For a material purchased externally (referred to as Buy) with a low
variability indicator and a short lead time, the VF is 0.30 and the LT factor is 0.80. This
is noted on the first row of the buffer profile in Figure 14.9.
© 

787
14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
If necessary, you can create a new buffer profile with adjusted factors or assign a differ-
ent buffer to each plant (in this case, Plant 1710, as shown in Figure 14.10). We will use
Plant 1710 as an example throughout this chapter.
Figure 14.10 Plant-Specific Settings of Buffer Profile
The next section discusses some of the key DDR terms, which are helpful to refer to
when considering Figure 14.9 and Figure 14.10.
Key Terms
The following list contains some important DDR terms and their meanings. These
terms are either defined in supply chain associations, such as the Association for Sup-
ply Chain Management, or by the Demand-Driven Institute:
 Item type
This categorizes various material types, such as procured (P), intermediate (I), semi-
finished (SF), manufactured (M), and distributed (D). In the SAP S/4HANA buffer pro-
file, procured materials are referred to as Buy, the produced components are
referred to as Make, and the interplant transfer (stock transport orders) of materials
are referred to as Transfer.
 Average daily usage (ADU)
This is the average consumption of a material in a given time period.
 Minimum order quantity (MOQ)
This is the minimum procurement quantity imposed by the supplier or the minimum
production quantity or economic order quantity of a material. In SAP S/4HANA, this
information is maintained in the Minimum Lot Size field in the MRP1 view of a material
master.
 Imposed or desired order cycle (DOC)
This is the frequency of procuring a material, either imposed from the supplier side
or a company’s own ordering cycle.
 Decoupled lead time (DLT)
This is the longest but reduced unprotected or unbuffered lead time in the BOM to
procure or produce a material. In SAP S/4HANA, this information is maintained or
updated in the Total Replenishment Lead Time field of a material master (in the MRP
3 view of a material master).

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
788
 Lead time factor (LT factor)
This denotes a factor in the lead time to procure or produce a material in the range
of 0.0–1.0. Longer lead times will have smaller LT factors and vice versa. Materials
planners should mutually agree to define the lead time categories (long, medium,
and short) and then assign an LT factor to each lead time category. Here’s an exam-
ple:
– Long lead time: LT factor = 0.20
– Medium lead time: LT factor = 0.50
– Short lead time: LT factor = 0.85
 Variability factor (VF)
This denotes the demand or supply variability of materials in the range of 0.0–1.0.
Higher variability in demand or supply will have a higher VF and vice versa. Materi-
als planners should mutually agree to define the VF categories (stable, intermittent,
and unstable/unpredictable demand/supply) and then assign a VF to each category.
Here’s an example:
– Erratic supply/demand (high variability): VF = 0.85
– Semi-stable supply/demand (significantly high variability): VF = 0.70
– Mostly stable supply/demand (medium variability): VF = 0.30
 Buffer profile
This is a logical grouping of materials that share similar characteristics, such as
materials being procured, manufactured, or distributed across warehouses. The buf-
fer profile also mentions the LT factor and VF of each material. A buffer profile con-
sists of an item part, a lead time category (and an LT factor), and a variability cate-
gory (and a VF). For example, a buffer profile of M, M (0.4), L (0.33) means the follow-
ing:
– The part is manufactured (M).
– The part has a medium lead time (M) with an LT factor (0.4).
– The part has low (L) demand/supply variability with a VF (0.33).
 On-hand alert threshold
This is defined as a percentage of the safety stock and is maintained in the Buffer
Profile Maintenance app (refer to the last column of Figure 14.10). For example, if the
safety stock of a material is 1,000 PC and an on-hand alert threshold is maintained
as 0.10 in the buffer profile, then the system will issue a red alert in the Replenish-
ment Execution by On-Hand Alert app as soon as the stock falls to 100 or below
(1,000 × 0.10 = 100 PC).
 Spike horizon
The spike horizon is a time window that starts the day after today and comprises the
DLT multiplied by a factor called the spike horizon DLT multiplier (SHT), with the
addition of an offset called the spike horizon constant. The idea is that DDR will also
© 

789
14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
react to a demand within this time window to ensure product availability. Referring
to Figure 14.10 again, and as an example, the Spike Horizon Constant is maintained
as 5.00 (days), while the Spike Horizon DLT Multiplier is maintained as 0.50. Now, if
the DLT calculated by the DDR process is eight days, then the calculation for Spike
Horizon is Spike horizon = 5 (spike horizon constant) + (8 (DLT) × 0.5) = 9 days.
 Spike threshold
DDR won’t react to all the demand within the spike horizon but only to qualified
demands. To be considered a qualified demand, a requirement quantity must be
greater than the spike threshold, which is calculated by multiplying the safety stock
by the spike threshold factor. Only those highlighted demands will be considered
qualified demands because they are within the spike horizon and above the spike
threshold. Let’s explore this with an example: A safety stock (red zone) of 1,000 is
calculated by the DDR process. A qualified spike demand of 1,500 is taken into
account by DDR because it’s greater than the order spike threshold of 1,500. An order
spike threshold is a multiplication of the safety stock (in this example, 1,000) by the
Spike Threshold (1.50) as defined in the buffer profile (refer to Figure 14.10). There-
fore, 1,000 × 1.5 = 1,500, which is the order spike threshold. So, any sales order with
quantity of 1,500 or more and that is within the spike horizon (nine days; see previ-
ous bullet point) is considered a qualified demand.
Material Requirements Planning Type
DDR uses the MRP type D1 that has Firming Type 1—Automatic Firming and Order Pro-
posals Rescheduled Out. This setting entails that any existing planned order that lies
within the time fence will be automatically considered firm and won’t be changed by
MRP. If there is a shortage within the time fence, a new planned order will be created,
but it will be rescheduled out (i.e., it will be created outside the time fence). As soon as
this newly created planned order falls within the planning time fence, the system will
automatically firm it.
If the planning time fence is 15 days, then in a stock/requirements list (Transaction
MD04), the MRP results when using MRP type P1 will uniquely identify the End of Plan-
ning Time Fence when this 15-day period ends. A DDMRP type can be any MRP type with
MRP procedure C, such as the standard MRP type D1, which will be used throughout this
chapter. A DDMRP type acts like a reorder point MRP type, wherein the yellow buffer
zone is the reorder point, the green zone represents the maximum stock level, and the
safety stock is the red zone.
Note
MRP type D1 can not only be used in discrete manufacturing but also in process manu-
facturing. For repetitive manufacturing, you should use the new MRP type D2 available
in SAP S/4HANA 2023.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
790
Lot Sizing
A lot sizing procedure defines the rules for the quantity of the replenishment proposal
created by MRP. The lot sizing procedures available in SAP S/4HANA can be grouped
into the following categories:
 Static lot sizing procedures
 Period lot sizing procedures
 Optimum lot sizing procedures
DDR belongs to the static lot sizing procedures, in which the quantity will be calculated
according to the quantity specifications defined in the material master. To achieve the
correct MRP results for DDR, it’s also important to choose a lot sizing procedure that
will replenish up to the maximum stock level after covering the requirements. For this,
standard lot sizing procedure HB (replenish up to maximum stock level) is available in
the system.
Positioning a buffer for a product in DDR entails selecting a DDMRP type, such as the
standard MRP type D1, as well as using standard lot sizing procedure HB so that MRP
can correctly replenish up to the maximum stock level. Technically, these settings can
be made directly in the material master, but SAP also offers the Buffer Positioning app
(discussed in Section 14.5.5), which was specially developed for this purpose. When
you’re using this application, filters are available so that you can select the products to
process. Those products will be listed with their respective classifications and further
details, such as the lead time and the DLT. You can use this information to help deter-
mine whether a buffer should be positioned or not.
Note
In SAP S/4HANA 2023, SAP introduced a new configuration setting (Transaction OMDQ)
to allow material planners to define additional requirements for inclusion in the net-
flow position (NFP) calculation using the MRP type for DDMRP procedure. Following are
the additional requirements categories that you can select for inclusion in open past
demand:
 Purchase requisition releases of stock transfer purchase requisitions
 Scheduling agreement delivery schedules for stock transfer schedule lines
 Subcontracting requirements (parts to be provided to supplier for adding value to a
product) of subcontracting purchase requisitions
 Dependent requirements of planned orders

sections.title: Master Data Settings
sections.text:
14.4.2 Master Data Settings
The following sections list the critical master data setup required for the DDR process.
© 

791
14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
Material Master
For material master 2269 in plant 1710, the MRP Type field is assigned “D1,” while the Lot
Sizing Procedure field is assigned “HB.” This material-plant combination will be used as
a DDR example throughout this chapter. Meanwhile, for the sake of this example only,
you should maintain the numeric values for different fields in the material master as
noted in Table 14.2, which also includes the purposes they serve.
Note
As this is just an example, you should simply maintain any reasonable values in all
stock-related fields. The DDR process will automatically calculate and update these
fields based on actual material usage or consumption data, which is discussed in Sec-
tion 14.4.3.
Second, for all the materials that are part of the DDR process, such as raw materials,
packing materials, and semifinished materials, you should maintain the relevant data
in the respective material masters.
Figure 14.11 shows the Demand-Driven Replenishment tab that’s available in the Stock/
Requirements List (Transaction MD04), in which the Net Flow Position is shown along
with other details. The Demand-Driven Replenishment tab will only appear in the
Stock/Requirements List for those materials that are DDR managed. Also, scroll to the
far-right side (by sliding the gray bar located at the center of the screen to the right side)
of the screen to see some more DDR classifications, such as the variability indicator and
the DLT indicator, among other information.
Fields in Material Master Field Values in
Material Master

Reorder Point “100” TOY
Safety Stock “50” TOR

For procured materials: value “F”

fer materials
Table 14.2 Material Master Fields Maintenance for Buffers in DDR

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
792
Figure 14.11 Stock/Requirements List for DDR Material
Bill of Materials
A material’s BOM consists of components grouped together with the relevant quanti-
ties that are needed to produce the material in the production process. For example, if
vanilla ice cream is the main product, then its BOM will consist of all the ingredients
(referred to as components) needed to produce it. Similarly, producing an automobile
requires an engine, a chassis, a body, and a large number of other components.
To create the BOM of a material, use Transaction CS01. On the initial screen, enter
Material “2269” and Plant “1710” and then maintain material numbers for the individ-
ual components, together with the quantity for each. Although it contains quite a num-
ber of materials, only materials 2262, 2265, and 2267 are relevant to DDR (in addition to
the main material, 2269).
Note
Although only BOM details are covered here, it’s also important to maintain the other
manufacturing data, such as the routing data for discrete manufacturing (the master
recipe for process manufacturing and rate routing for repetitive manufacturing) and
the production version. This master data setup will ensure that the planning elements
created by DDR can be successfully converted into production elements (e.g., planned
orders created in DDR can be converted into production or manufacturing orders).
Similarly, for procured materials, you should at least maintain a purchasing info record,
which is a one-to-one procurement relationship between a material and a supplier.
This master data setup will ensure that the planning elements created in DDR can be
successfully converted into procurement elements (e.g., purchase requisitions created
in DDR can be converted into purchase orders).
© 

793
14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
For interplant transfer, you maintain the material masters in question in both the plant
setting and the DDR-related setting, as already discussed in this earlier section.

sections.title: Transactions
sections.text:
14.4.3 Transactions
To trigger and calculate the average daily usage (ADU) of DDR-relevant materials, let’s
perform some GI and GR transactions beforehand in the system and on different dates.
For example, you can do the following:
1. Receive four materials in a storage location in different quantities and on different
dates in the past against the initial stock posting.
2. Issue out (consume) the materials against a cost center in different quantities and on
different dates in the past so that the system can automatically determine the ADU.
3. Manually create a few sales orders for this material on different dates in the past and
also in the future.
4. As DDR now also enables planning for the future to account for any forecast/pre-
dicted requirements, you should maintain some values in the PIRs. Performing this
step will enable the system to calculate the ADU for future consumption too.
All these steps are briefly covered in the following sections.
Goods Receipts/Goods Issue
In this example, when the DDR and non-DDR materials are created in the SAP S/4HANA
system, there are no initial stocks or any history of past receipts or consumption. To
maintain some stocks of different materials on different dates in the past, you perform
the GR process.
Use Transaction MIGO, choose Goods Receipt from the first dropdown list, and then
choose Other from the second dropdown list. Then, you ensure that movement type
561 is used for initial entry of stock balances (Init.entry of stBal.), as shown on the right-
hand side of the screen.
In this example, four DDR-based materials are received in a quantity of 100 each on July
15, 2024 (the Posting Date). A few more similar GR transactions are undertaken but in
varying quantities and on different dates. These receipts of materials on different dates
and in different quantities will enable the system to calculate the LT factor and supply VF.
In a real-time, actual supply chain scenario, such GRs are undertaken with reference to
purchase orders for procured materials, via production or process orders for in-house-
produced materials, and via stock transport orders for interplant transfer materials.
To begin GI—that is, to record consumption of materials with reference to a cost
center—you make selections from both the dropdowns: choose Goods Issue from the
first dropdown on the left-hand side and then select Other in the relevant options drop-
down that appears. Then, make sure to use movement type 201 in the GI for Cost Center

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
794
field for consumption of materials against the cost center, as shown on the right-hand
side of the screen. You can then undertake a few more similar GIs, but in different
quantities and on different dates. These issuances and consumptions of materials on
different dates and in different quantities will enable the system to calculate the ADU.
In the real-time supply chain scenario, goods are issued with reference to production or
process orders (via movement type 261), cost centers (via movement type 201), sale
orders (via movement type 601), or stock transport orders (via movement type 541).
Note
DDMRP considers all goods movement, such as goods issue and goods receipt, during
the period under evaluation. But if necessary, you can exclude certain goods issuance,
such as goods issue against scrap (via movement type 551), from the DDMRP calcula-
tion. To make the relevant configuration settings, you use menu path SAP IMG  Pro-
duction  Demand-Driven Replenishment  Assignment of Profile for Goods Issue
Selection (DD).
Planned Independent Requirements
You can use PIRs to project ADU in the future and allow the system to calculate a pro-
jected buffer for the future. This is especially relevant, for example, if you know in
advance that there will be an event that might affect product consumption, such as a
marketing campaign or a seasonal increase in demand. We’ll also cover this scenario in
this chapter. You can use the Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) app to define the
horizon in the future for which the system will look for PIRs.
To create the PIRs of a material, use either Transaction MD61 or MD64. For this example,
enter Material “2269” and Plant “1710” and assign the Version as “00” (Requirements Plan).
Define the Planning Horizon and the Planning period in days so that the system brings up
the relevant fields for data input. Then, press (Enter) to arrive at the screen shown in
Figure 14.12, which shows the day-wise future demand of material 2269 in plant 1710.
Figure 14.12 Planned Independent Requirements
© 

795
14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
It’s important to check the box in column A (active) so that the system will consider
these PIRs during the MRP run.
Open Past Demand
Open past demand refers to sales orders for which the delivery dates have already
passed but that aren’t delivered yet. To create open past demand—that is, to create a
sales order—use Transaction VA01. Enter the standard sales Order Type “OR” as well as
the organizational data details, such as Sales Organization “1000,” Distribution Chan-
nel “10,” and Division “00.” Then, press (Enter).
Then, as shown in Figure 14.13, you enter the customer, which in this case is Sold-to Party
“USCU S09.” At the item level, enter Material “2269” and an Order Quantity of “600” PC.
Figure 14.13 Sales Order
Although it’s not shown here, the Req. Deliv.Date (required delivery date) in this case is
07/14/2024 (July 14, 2024). This is the date that DDR will consider when evaluating both
the open past demand (sales orders that aren’t partially or fully delivered to customers
yet) and any future demand that lies within the spike horizon, to make them a part of
the net flow equation.
Next, you save the sales order, and, for this example, the system creates sales order
number 5616. If there are several line items in a sales order, then the system considers
the First Date field for scheduling and DDR calculation purposes. It’s important to note
that DDR only consider sales orders for demand and not customer requirements (via
Transaction MD81).

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
796
Open Orders
Open orders are basically the production orders or process orders for in-house-produced
materials, purchase orders for procurement-related materials, and stock transport orders
for interplant transfer-related materials whose goods are partially delivered or not deliv-
ered yet but are expected to be (i.e., open orders).
To create a production order, you use Transaction CO01 (use Transaction COR1 for cre-
ating a process order). On the initial production order screen that appears, enter Mate-
rial “2269,” production Plant “1710,” and standard order Type “PP10.” Then, choose Con-
tinue.
The header data screen of the production order consists of information that is applica-
ble to the entire order, such as the production order number, material number, plant,
and order type. The Total Qty field is relevant, and the quantity of 100 is the open order
quantity of material 2269 in plant 1710 that hasn’t been delivered yet and hence is con-
sidered in the net flow equation of DDR. The Dates/Times section is equally important
because these dates form the basis for when the system expects the goods to be deliv-
ered to the warehouse.
On the production order screen, you navigate to Components, which shows the BOM
data and the components that will be used in the production process.
Reservations
The system has automatically created reservations for three components of main
material 2269—including for components 2262, 2265, and 2267—along with relevant
quantities. Movement type 261, which denotes the consumption of this component,
will be recorded against this production order. Finally, as these three components are
also DDR-related materials, DDR will use the net flow equation.
The following sections will discuss how to run the DDR process in SAP S/4HANA to
ensure that you protect throughput, classify products, calculate and update buffers,
and ensure better planning and execution while also focusing on key DDR metrics and
analytics.

sections.title: Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
sections.text:
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
The main purpose of maintaining buffers is to provide a cushion against the shock of
fluctuations in supply chain management and financial activity by protecting the
throughput. Throughput is defined as the rate at which material exits the manufactur-
ing process. Lead time is the time it takes to move through the manufacturing process,
and WIP, as mentioned previously, is the amount of inventory contained between entry
and exit points of the manufacturing process. Therefore, throughput = (WIP ÷ lead time).
© 

797
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
The following sections will discuss how to protect throughput, classify materials based
on ABC, PQR, and XYZ position buffers, and classify materials based on lead time.

sections.title: Protecting Throughput
sections.text:
14.5.1 Protecting Throughput
DDR builds on the same buffer concept to achieve the following two primary objec-
tives:
 It provides shock absorption by providing inventory, thereby ensuring the protec-
tion of downstream users from fluctuations in demand and supply.
 It reduces the lead time required to deliver a product by removing anything that’s
upstream of the buffer from the cumulative lead time.
To achieve these objectives, you undertake a series of DDR-specific steps in SAP
S/4HANA, such as classifying products for variability or usage value, positioning buf-
fers, and finally classifying products based on the lead time.
Classification is a central step in determining the decoupling points in a multilevel
BOM structure as part of the DDR approach. You valuate the planning objects’ product/
plant or product/plant/MRP areas according to their essential characteristics and then
assign them to classes on this basis. This classification helps you as a planner identify
the appropriate settings for each planning object, which you should select based on
these characteristics.
In the context of DDR, these settings are defined in a certain way. The results of classifi-
cation lead to the assignment of buffer profiles in a subsequent step: buffer positioning.
As a result, classification is a central building block in the planning process of the DDR
approach in SAP S/4HANA. In SAP S/4HANA, classification is carried out exclusively
based on SAP Fiori apps.

sections.title: Classification
sections.text:
14.5.2 Classification
The Schedule Product Classification (DD) app, along with other DDR-related apps, is
available to you after you activate DDR in SAP S/4HANA. These apps are available via
the SAP Fiori launchpad.
You need to execute some SAP Fiori apps for DDR when you start using DDR as a solu-
tion, whereas you’ll use operational SAP Fiori apps for DDR on a daily basis. This section
will walk you through the DDR implementation steps, beginning with classifying the
products. Technically speaking, buffer positioning may take place before classification,
but product classification is an important factor for you to consider when you’re decid-
ing where to position a buffer, so you as a materials planner should always start with
product classification.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
798
Note
Because product classification will help you identify where to position a buffer, we rec-
ommend that you classify all the products that may potentially be planned with the
DDR logic.
After you open the app, an overview screen appears and displays the job runs of the
past. Choose Create to start an application job for making the necessary settings for the
classification run. The Job Template in Figure 14.14 1 is preselected, while you can
maintain a user-defined Job Name to clearly distinguish the individual application
jobs. Scroll down and then check the Start Immediately 2 box to begin the application
job right away. To schedule an application job, choose Define Recurrence Pattern,
which opens the popup shown in Figure 14.15. When you’re scheduling a new job, it’s
important to first define the scheduling options that will set the job start date and the
recurrence pattern.
Figure 14.14 Product Classification
While you’re choosing the relevant option from the Recurrence Pattern dropdown
scheduling options shown in Figure 14.15, the next few fields will prompt you to main-
tain the application job recurrence’s details. For this example, choose Single Run and
then choose OK, and the screen in Figure 14.16 will appear.
© 

799
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
Figure 14.15 Scheduling Options
Figure 14.16 Selection Criteria for Products
After the scheduling step, the next step is to define the Selection Criteria settings that
will be used to select which products will be classified in the job. Figure 14.16 shows four
entries in the Product field and the relevant Plant as selection criteria, so the applica-
tion job will classify all the products that meet these criteria.
An important parameter included in the selection criteria is the Number of Days (Past)
value. This will represent the number of days in the past during which the system will
look for goods movements (goods issuances/consumptions) to calculate the ADU. In
this example, the Number of Days (Past) value is maintained as “30,” so the system will
look for goods issued in the past 30 days to determine the ADU. If needed, you can
choose to exclude days when there was no goods issuance by checking the Exclude Non
Goods Issue Days box (see Figure 14.17). Doing so will increase the ADU of the DDR
materials under consideration.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
800
Scrolling down the Selection Criteria section in Figure 14.16 will bring up the screen area
shown in Figure 14.17, where you can define the thresholds for the value, BOM usage,
and variability classifications.
As this app analyzes material movements to determine past consumption during the
specified period, you can now influence the selection of material documents that form
the basis of consumption. You can influence the threshold values that are used to
delimit the previously mentioned classes and maintain them as percentage values in
the app, as shown in Figure 14.17.
Next, you maintain the relevant classification parameters for ABC, XYZ, and PQR, as
shown in Figure 14.17. Then, choose the Check button to validate all the settings you’ve
made, and the message noted at the bottom of the screen will appear. Finally, choose
Schedule to schedule the application job.
Figure 14.17 Thresholds Settings
You can make settings for three different classification procedures:
 ABC analysis
 XYZ analysis
 PQR analysis
The ABC classification evaluates the relative importance of a material/plant or material/
plant/MRP area combination in relation to the other planning objects analyzed on the
basis of historical consumption. The objects are divided into three groups:
 A materials
Relative to all objects analyzed in a run, these have had the highest consumption value
in the past.
 B materials
These materials have a lower proportion of total consumption than A materials.
© 

801
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
 C materials
These materials have the lowest proportion of total consumption.
For classification, the consumption value is determined on the basis of the material
documents. The materials involved in the classification run are then sorted in descend-
ing order according to their consumption value. An A is assigned to a planning object
until the threshold value of, for example, 70% is reached cumulatively across all mate-
rials of the class. The subsequent materials are then assigned a B classification until,
say, the next 20% of the consumption value is reached; the remaining materials are
classified as C materials. The Schedule Product Classification (DD) app can perform this
analysis at the level of material/plant combinations, as well as at the level of material/
plant MRP area; other levels aren’t possible.
You’ll use the Value (ABC) Classification setting to define whether a product belongs to
the group of products with the largest GI value (classification A), to the intermediate
group with a medium GI value (classification B), or to the group of products with the
lowest GI value (classification C). SAP S/4HANA will calculate the total GI value of these
products within the interval defined in the selection criteria, sort materials according
to this value, and then define to which group each product belongs, according to the
percentages defined at these thresholds. This means that the input for these thresholds
will be a percentage, and the sum of the three values should be 100%.
SAP S/4HANA will calculate the total GI value within the period; that is, it will consider
both the material price and the number of issues. This means that a material with a
high price but without too many GIs may have the same classification as a material
with a low price and a high number of GIs.
ABC analysis is therefore a way of dividing the planning objects into classes according
to their value-based significance. This analysis method is now being combined with
two other methods that bring in further important aspects for the identification of key
characteristics, thus facilitating your goal of being able to assign optimal settings to a
planning object (for example, with regard to buffer configuration). One of the two addi-
tional classification methods provided by the app is XYZ analysis, which is used to
assess the predictability of future demand on the basis of past consumption. Analo-
gous to ABC analysis, there are three classes:
 X materials
These materials have the most constant consumption, so it’s assumed that future
consumption can be predicted with high accuracy and that therefore, buffers for the
coverage of uncertainties can be reduced.
 Y materials
The materials in this class have greater variation in consumption than X materials.
 Z materials
These materials show the greatest variation in consumption, so it’s assumed that
predictability accuracy is low.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
802
The criterion for assessing predictability is a statistical fluctuation factor called the
coefficient of variation, which determines, squares, and sums up the deviation of each
individual observed historical value from the mean value. This sum is then divided by
the number of observed values and normalized to the mean value to obtain a relative
fluctuation measure.
As with ABC analysis, the app enables the maintenance of threshold limits for XYZ
analysis. Unlike in ABC analysis, however, these are absolute values, not percentage val-
ues.
The Variability (XYZ) Classification setting will classify products according to the
demand variability. Products with a classification of X will have a low variability, prod-
ucts with a classification of Y will have medium variability, and products with a classi-
fication of Z will have high variability. The total sum of sales orders overdue, sales
orders due on the current date, and order spikes are considered as the actual demand.
With the mean and standard deviation of the actual demand, a coefficient of variation
is calculated. This coefficient is used as the threshold for variability: the higher the coef-
ficient, the higher the variability.
The last possible classification methodology within the framework of the app is the
PQR analysis, which you can use to analyze the planning objects to determine how
many BOMs they are used in. The reason for using this type of analysis is to find out
whether there is a single use or multiple uses and thus how many recipients are rele-
vant for the object.
You use the BOM Usage (PQR) Classification setting to determine whether a material is
used in a high number of BOMs (classification P), a medium number of BOMs (classifi-
cation Q), or a low number of BOMs (classification R). The value defined for each thresh-
old is basically the total number of material BOMs in which a product is used, and it will
be classified accordingly.
You can use the three classification methods we’ve mentioned here to classify material/
plant combinations or material/plant/MRP area combinations according to the follow-
ing essential characteristics:
 How important they are in relative terms in the material spectrum
 How predictable the future demand is
 How many objects are used
You can then use these three characteristics to determine the central settings of the
DDR process, mainly for the purpose of defining how these objects are controlled and
whether you need to maintain inventory for them. Therefore, the results of the classi-
fication will play an important role in the next step: buffer positioning.
You can view a summary of the results of the classification run in the application log of
the Schedule Product Classification (DD) app. You can assess the log directly on the ini-
© 

803
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
tial screen of the app or by choosing the Back button in the job settings overview. From
the overview screen, you can access two more detailed views:
1. Job Log
This shows messages that have been identified by the system during runtime. In this
context, it’s also possible to filter by message types.
2. Job Details
This provides the settings selected for the job, such as threshold values and selec-
tions.
EFG Analysis According to Replenishment Lead Times
In SAP S/4HANA, another option for classifying objects is EFG classification, and it’s
based on the replenishment lead time. However, before you decide how to classify an
object on the basis of the replenishment lead time, you need to calculate the decou-
pled replenishment lead time, which doesn’t exist at this early stage. Therefore, you
can only execute this type of classification with a different app in a later process step.
We’ll discuss this topic in the context of lead time determination in Section 14.5.4.
The results of the classification are often used together with other information to make
the previously mentioned decisions, which will play a role at a later stage. If, in addition
to the mathematically deductible criteria of the classifications, you use additional cri-
teria that can’t be mathematically derived to make appropriate decisions, it’s referred
to as a grouping. For example, it can make sense to treat a simple standard part differ-
ently from a more complex electronic part with a similar consumption behavior—that
is, with comparable classification results. Classes and groups that are combined are
often called clusters.
After you finish the first step of product classification, the information you need to posi-
tion the decoupling buffers of the DDR products becomes available. To position those
buffers, you can consider the results of the previous classification to determine whether
a product is a good candidate for a buffer. The product classification, however, isn’t the
only factor you should consider: there are external factors, and you as a planner can also
use your own experience to determine when a buffer is necessary. There are some situ-
ations in which a buffer may be a good option:
 A semifinished product is classified as P because it’s used in too many BOMs.
 Because it’s a key component and it’s used to produce many products, it might be a
good idea to position a buffer for this product, thus avoiding stock-outs that might
have far-reaching effects.
 The source of supply of a given component purchased externally isn’t reliable, and
there are too many delayed deliveries. In this case, adding a buffer would mean that
the demand for this product would be absorbed by the safety stock if there is a delay.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
804
 An important product has a long lead time for production, so any late demand arriv-
ing would mean a long delay.
 In an MTO scenario, a customer can’t wait for the whole total replenishment lead
time to have the product delivered. In this case, adding a buffer to strategically
selected components will reduce the lead time, as the demand from this customer
will be absorbed by the buffer.

sections.title: Buffer Positioning
sections.text:
14.5.3 Buffer Positioning
When entering this application, you use the filters to select which products will be pro-
cessed. Those products will be listed with their respective classifications and further
details, such as the lead time and the DLT. You can use this information to help deter-
mine whether to position a buffer or not (see Figure 14.18). Furthermore, you can select
materials and choose Simulate DLT to simulate the decoupled lead times of these DDR
materials.
Note
Notice the two tabs in Figure 14.18: Positioning -Internal and Positioning-External. You
can use the former to determine whether the buffer position and the buffer sizing will
be internal, that is, whether it will be executed within the SAP S/4HANA. You can use
the latter to determine whether it will be external—that is, executed by an external
system.
Figure 14.18 Unbuffered Products without Decoupled Lead Time
© 

805
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
You can directly buffer or unbuffer a product on the initial screen of the Buffer Posi-
tioning app by choosing the Buffer or Unbuffer button (see Figure 14.18). When you
select one of these options, a popup appears to select a new MRP type, the new lot siz-
ing procedure, and additional DDR-related material master settings. Any changes you
make here will be saved directly in the material master. From the initial screen, you can
also simulate the DLT calculation for one or more products, as is done in this example
and shown in Figure 14.19 in the Simulated DLT column. You can then analyze the dif-
ference between the current DLT and the proposed DLT for informational purposes and
even for decision-making purposes.
Figure 14.19 Simulated Decoupled Lead Time
The Simulated DLT column in Figure 14.19 shows that the DLT isn’t calculated for these
four selected products. When you select these four products and then choose Simulate
DLT, the simulated DLT (in days) will appear as shown in Figure 14.19.
Before you decide whether to position a buffer for a specific product or not, you should
select a specific product (in this example, it is material 2262), and navigate to the Buffer
Analysis app, where extensive details are available to support you in making your deci-
sion (see Figure 14.20). To navigate from the Buffer Positioning app (refer to Figure
14.19) to the Buffer Analysis app, select the relevant material, which in this example is
material 2262, and choose the forward arrow icon located on the far-right side of an
individual material. The screen shown in Figure 14.20 then appears.
Figure 14.20 shows all the information related to the Upstream and Downstream val-
ues. In this situation, upstream refers to product components, for which there are the
longest path or the whole BOM. Downstream refers to product parents, for which the
system displays information that is related to all the BOMs in which a product is used
(see Figure 14.21).

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
806
Figure 14.20 Buffer Analysis
Figure 14.21 Upstream
To analyze a product with many levels of components, we recommend that you switch
to the Network Graph view (see Figure 14.21), where there is a graphical representation
of the upstream and downstream values and multiple display options, as shown in
Figure 14.22.
In Figure 14.22, it’s possible to show, for example, the longest path, the product flow, or
the entire BOM and BOM usage from the first dropdown, which is located in the top-left
corner of the screen. There are different options to highlight specific products in the
network, such as buffered products, products under an area of responsibility (AOR),
products selected as favorites, and products in one of the product classifications.
The example in Figure 14.23 shows a partial view of the whole BOM of a product (2269)
with the characteristic (DLT Indicator) chosen from the dropdown in Figure 14.22. The
BOM components are highlighted in red, yellow, or green, depending on the BOM usage.

sections.title: Lead Time Classification
sections.text:
14.5.4 Lead Time Classification
The Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products (DD) app uses the DDR planning
characteristics maintained in the previous step of buffer positioning to determine the
DLTs. These DLTs are the basis for buffer level determination.
The second main task of the Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products (DD) app is
to carry out a fourth classification method: the EFG classification by replenishment
lead time. Unlike the three classification methods explained earlier, this classification
can only be carried out after the DDR-dispatching characteristics have been main-
tained because the decoupling points aren’t available in the system at the beginning of
the process.
After you call up the Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products (DD) app, a selection
screen appears where you can create a new analysis run. In addition to the job name
and start time, it’s important for you to define the objects—for example, Product, Prod-
uct Group, Plant, and MRP Controller—for which the system should perform the analy-
sis. Another central setting is the horizon for historical replenishment (see Number of
Days (Past) in Figure 14.24).
Figure 14.24 Job Settings for EFG Analysis
© 

809
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
Figure 14.24 shows an overview of the possible settings regarding the classification
thresholds of the EFG analysis.
This app classifies the products according to the lead time (EFG) classification. Just as in
the Schedule Product Classification app, the initial screen of this app will show an over-
view of the jobs that are already completed, in process, and scheduled. On this screen,
you can create a new job, copy an existing job, or check the results of the finished jobs.
The thresholds defined here are only for the EFG classification, but a materials planner
can define different thresholds depending on the procurement type. The following
options are available:
 DLT Threshold for Buy
This is used for products procured externally—that is, with procurement type F.
 DLT Threshold for Make
This will be considered for products manufactured internally—that is, products with
procurement type E.
 DLT Threshold for Transfer
This is used for products with a stock transfer special procurement type, which are
supplied by means of a stock transfer from another plant.
In these thresholds, you as a materials planner can define how many days is considered
a short, medium, or long lead time for products manufactured, purchased, or trans-
ferred. This differentiation of thresholds depending on the procurement type happens
because the lead time to manufacture a product may be very different from the lead
time to buy the components.
In addition, you must specify appropriate thresholds for the EFG classification. This clas-
sification methodology is executed for material/plant combinations with a DDMRP
type based on their DLTs, which are also determined in this run together with the single-
level replenishment lead times. You can use different threshold values in calendar days
for in-house production, external procurement, and stock transfers. In all three cases,
the materials are divided into three groups:
 E materials
Materials with short replenishment lead times according to the respective value
definition
 F materials
Materials with average replenishment lead times according to the threshold value
definition
 G materials
Materials with long replenishment lead times according to the threshold value defi-
nition

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
810
While the EFG classification and, by its very nature, the determination of the DLTs is
only carried out for decoupled materials with a DDMRP type, the single-level replenish-
ment lead times are determined for all selected objects, including those for which no
buffering is planned.
For in-house production, the system determines the production or process orders over
the selected analysis horizon. For all identified objects, the time period between order
creation and final confirmation is determined as a single-level replenishment lead
time and then aggregated as an average value on the material/plant combination. If no
element is found within the selected horizon, the system uses the in-house production
time of the material master, which is independent of lot size.
The procedure for external procurement is the same, which means the purchase orders
within the horizon are selected accordingly, and the period between purchase order
creation and the date on which the final delivery indicator is set is determined. As is
true for in-house production, there is a fallback scenario for external procurement in
case no element is found within the selected horizon. In this instance, the system first
uses the planned delivery time of the info record or contract that the MRP run selects
as the preferred source of supply according to the settings. The system uses an explo-
sion with lot size 1 and the explosion date of the run. If the system can’t determine a
replenishment lead time, it uses the planned delivery time of the material master.
For interplant transfer materials, the system evaluates the time when the goods/mate-
rials were issued from a supplying plant against a stock transport order until these
goods were received at the receiving plant.
The determination of the DLTs is based on the single-level replenishment lead times
determined as described earlier, and it uses the multilevel sum of the longest critical
path in each case.
The system also delivers an active BAdI implementation that enables the automated
maintenance of the values in the material master. Maintenance takes place in the mas-
ter data fields that were specified previously as a fallback option for determining
replenishment lead times—that is, into the lot-size-independent in-house-production
time for in-house production and into the planned delivery time of the preferred
source of supply or the material master in the case of external procurement. The DLT is
updated in the Tot. Repl. Lead Time (total replenishment lead time) field (in the MRP 3
view) of the material master.

sections.title: Buffer Level Determination
sections.text:
14.5.5 Buffer Level Determination
You now use these areas to derive buffer levels. The following areas are distinguished:
 TOR
This area marks the upper end of the red buffer zone.
© 

811
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
 TOY
This buffer level is the sum of the red and yellow buffer zones.
 TOG
This buffer level is the sum of the red, yellow, and green buffer zones.
If the inventory position is lower than TOY, a replenishment to TOG will be triggered in
the planning run. If the stock is above TOG, it’s in the blue zone. This depiction of the
buffer zones and their levels is shown in Figure 14.25.
Figure 14.25 Buffer Zones and Levels in DDR
The following sections will discuss how to schedule buffer level calculation, position
buffers, evaluate buffer levels and average daily usage, and adjust buffers.
Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation
The last step in DDR implementation is the actual buffer calculation, which you can
also schedule as a background job by using the Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation
app. On the initial screen, there are options to manage the already scheduled and fin-
ished jobs and to schedule a new background job execution. The selection screen pro-
vides the scheduling parameters and allows you to select which products should be
included in the buffer calculation, as shown in Figure 14.26.
The Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation app performs the calculation of the buffer
zones according to the DDR logic, which means that it calculates the central buffer
zones and levels. After calling up the Schedule Buffer Level Calculation app, you as a
planner can configure an analysis run, including selecting whether to calculate the
ADU—that is, the average demand per day. It’s based on the previous step of classifica-
tion, which used the material movements (GIs and GRs). Figure 14.26 shows the possi-
ble settings in the Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation app. You also need to specify
here whether the results should be saved or just adopted within the defined tolerances.
