14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
812
Furthermore, you can choose the option to reuse or recalculate the DLT from the mate-
rial master by selecting the relevant radio button located in the bottom-right corner of
the screen.
Figure 14.26 Schedule Buffer Level Calculation App
There are also options to choose whether the buffer will be adopted only if the new value
is within a tolerance interval (Adopt with Tolerance), whether it will be always adopted
(Always Adopt Proposals), or whether it will never be adopted (Do Not Adopt Proposals).
If you choose either Adopt with Tolerance or Do Not Adopt Proposals, you’ll then need
to review the calculated buffer later in the Manage Buffer Levels app. Furthermore, you
can choose whether the ADU will be calculated based on a fixed interval or on a rolling
interval and whether the DLT will come from the material master values or will be recal-
culated. If needed, you can choose to exclude the days when there was no goods issu-
ance by checking the Exclude Non Goods Issue Days box. Doing so will increase the ADU
of the DDR materials under consideration.
For this example, choose Do Not Adopt Proposals. After you choose Check and then
finally Schedule, the system will create a log for the scheduled job.
Note
You can also use the PIRs to project an ADU in the future and allow the system to also
calculate a projected buffer for the future. This is especially relevant, for example, if
you know in advance that an event—such as a marketing campaign or a seasonal
© 

813
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
increase in demand—might affect product consumption. You can define the future
horizon during which the system will look for planned independent requirements in
the Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) app, which we discuss later in Section 14.5.5.
On the initial screen, a flag will appear in the Log column for jobs already completed,
and it will show the status of the background job execution. If you double-click this flag,
the log details will appear, including any warning or error messages that may prevent
the buffer calculation.
Position Buffers
Next, you use the Manage Buffer Levels app to evaluate the results. Figure 14.27 shows
an example of buffer level management by manual intervention. The system proposes
that you increase the Current stock of 500 PC to Proposed 1160 PC. The Buffer Levels
field first shows the current buffer levels and then shows the proposed buffer levels
directly below that (in a tricolor horizontal bar). The Average Daily Usage (Historical
and Future) column provides insight into the ADU of material 2269, in the yellow zone
(96.667 PC). The lower half of the screen shows the zone-wise suggestions for the pro-
posed buffers, and we’ll discuss them in detail soon.
Figure 14.27 Buffer Calculation
To analyze material 2269. choose the forward arrow located on the far-right side of the
screen to go to the next screen shown in Figure 14.28.
While selecting a specific DDR product in this application, you can scroll down into a
more detailed view of the buffer, ADU, and DLT, as shown in various tabs in Figure 14.28.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
814
Figure 14.28 shows the current lower buffer levels and the system-proposed buffers to
be maintained on a daily basis. If you choose a horizontal bar on a specific date, it will
reveal the date and details of the respective zones, which, in this example, are that as of
July 19, 2024, the Yellow Zone is 483 PC, the Green Zone is 242 PC, and the Red Zone is
435 PC.
Figure 14.28 Planning View of Redefined Buffer Levels
If you select another view from the Planning View dropdown, such as the Comparison
View, the system will show comparisons of various stock levels. The Settings icon lets
you switch between viewing the details as zones or as stocks, while using the Legends
icon provides details of this visual representation. These icons are located in the top-
right corner of the screen.
Buffer Levels
You can review the calculated buffer levels in the Manage Buffer Levels app. When you
enter this application, you’ll see a list of products and the respective buffer levels calcu-
lated by the system, including a graphical representation of the historic and projected
buffer levels and the ADU. Scroll down the screen shown in Figure 14.28 until the screen
shown in Figure 14.29 appears (or choose the Average Daily Usage tab in Figure 14.28).
© 

815
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
Average Daily Usage
The blue bars in Figure 14.29 represent the ADU during the past 30 days, based on actual
GIs. The orange bar represents future ADU based on the planned independent require-
ments maintained for material 2269 in plant 1710, as well as the settings maintained in
the screen for mass adjustments (shown later in Figure 14.34). The past and future hori-
zons are also shown as Horizon for Past and Horizon for Future, respectively, in the top-
left corner of Figure 14.29.
Figure 14.29 Past and Future Average Daily Usage
Classifications
Figure 14.30 shows any proposed changes in classifications, such as, DLT, BOM usage,
and VF, among other options. Here, the DLT for Proposed Buffer is 5.00 Days, and a
Change in DLT of 4.00 Days is proposed.
Figure 14.30 Decoupled Lead Time
Figure 14.31 is the summary of all the current versus proposed changes (if any) to the
classification of a DDR-based material. Here, the Proposed Variability Factor is 0.80 and
the Proposed DLT Factor is 0.50.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
816
Figure 14.31 Classifications
Buffer Adjustments
You also can switch to edit mode and make manual adjustments to the calculated buf-
fer level by adding an adjustment factor for each zone. Then, the value originally calcu-
lated for each zone will be multiplied by this factor within the period defined for this
buffer adjustment. After entering edit mode, you select the Show Adjustments option
to view all the existing zone adjustments per period. There’s also an option to add a
new adjustment or delete an existing one. Figure 14.32 shows an example in which an
adjustment was created from July 14 to July 27, 2024, with factors of 1.50, 2.50, and 0.80
for the red, yellow, and green zones, respectively. When you add a new zone adjust-
ment, choose Simulate Changes to see the changes reflected in the chart.
Based on the information and data that are available, let’s first summarize the classifi-
cation of material 2269 undertaken through the DDR process and then understand how
the three buffer zones were calculated. All data and calculations are based on a date of
July 18, 2024.
 ADU = 96.667 PC
 DLT = 5 Days
 VF = 0.8
 DLT Factor = 0.5
Therefore, on July 19, 2024, the yellow zone = ADU × DLT = 96.667 × 5 = 483 PC, which is
rounded off because the unit of measure is PC (pieces), and the calculated value is dis-
played without decimal places. You can validate this on the screen shown in Figure
14.28.
© 

817
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
Figure 14.32 Manual Adjustment of Buffer Levels
The green zone is calculated as the yellow zone × DLT factor = 483 × 0.5 = 242 PC, which
you can validate in Figure 14.28.
Because the red zone consists of a red base and a red safety, let’s calculate them first:
 Red base = Yellow zone × DLT factor = 483 × 0.5 = 241.5 PC
 Red safety = Red base × VF = 241.5× 0.8 = 193 PC
 Red zone = Red base + red safety = 241 + 193 = 435 PC (You can validate this in Figure
14.28.)
Next, let’s discuss how these three calculated zones update the various fields in the
material master (or product):
 TOR = Safety stock or the red zone = 435 PC (see the Safety stock (DD) field in Figure
14.33 1)
 TOY = TOR + yellow zone = 435 + 483 = 918 PC (see the Reorder Point (DD) field in Figure
14.33 1)
 TOG = TOY + green zone = 918 + 242 = 1,160 PC (see the Maximum Stock (DD) field in
Figure 14.33 1)
Note that the data in Figure 14.33 1 is only updated after you adopt the buffer proposal
by choosing Save and Adopt on the screen shown in Figure 14.30. Adopting a buffer pro-
posal automatically updates the relevant fields of the material master with updated
values. These fields are Safety Stock, Reorder Point, Maximum Stock, and Total Replen-
ishment Lead Time.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
818
Note
Because product characteristics, such as lead time or usage, may change over time, it’s
a best practice to periodically review the product classifications. In addition, you should
also periodically review the buffer and recalculate it because the ADU varies over time.
For this, you can use the scheduling options available in the relevant DDR apps.
Once you’ve adopted the buffer proposal, the next step is to run MRP Live (Transaction
MD01N) so that the system creates replenishment proposals. After MRP Live runs, the
stock/requirements list (Transaction MD04) is updated accordingly, as shown in Figure
14.33 (see the upper half of the stock requirements list 1). All stock fields, such as Max-
imum Stock, Reorder Point, and Safety Stock, are updated along with other demand-
driven fields. Recall that all these fields were blank when the DDR process wasn’t initi-
ated on material 2269 and for plant 1710 in Section 14.3.
Figure 14.33 Buffer Levels Redefined
More fields and associated calculations shown in Figure 14.33 2 are discussed in the
next section.
Finally, when a buffer proposal is created and adopted, the system sets the following
values as defaults for a product:
 Individual lead time = (Planned delivery duration or in-house production duration,
based on the procurement type) + GR processing time
© 

819
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
 DLT = Individual lead time or total replenishment lead time
 ADU = (Reorder point – safety stock) ÷ DLT
 LT factor = (Maximum stock – reorder point) ÷ (reorder point – safety stock)
 VF = (Safety stock ÷ (maximum stock – reorder point)) – 1
As a self-learning DDR exercise, you can use the safety stock (545), reorder point (1,151),
maximum stock (1,453), and decoupled lead time (eight days) data to calculate and val-
idate the ADU, LT factor, and VF of 75.7, 0.5, and 0.8, respectively, from the preceding
three equations.
It’s also important to note that running MRP Live is one way of replenishing the stock.
The next section shows how to interactively manage all DDR planning with SAP Fiori
apps without needing to run MRP Live.
Mass Adjust Buffers
In some situations, products can’t be classified because there isn’t enough information
in the system. For example, in a new implementation, in which there’s a lack informa-
tion about the past consumption of products, the system can’t classify a product
according to the value or the variability. The same thing happens during the introduc-
tion of a new product, as there isn’t enough information available to classify the prod-
uct. In this case, we suggest that you use the Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) app to
manually set the product classification.
Within this application, you can manage mass changes for any of the product classifi-
cations by selecting products and choosing the Change button shown in Figure 14.34.
Figure 14.34 Mass Maintenance of Products

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
820
You can also change additional DDR-related fields, such as MRP Type, Maximum Stock
Level, Reorder Point, and Safety Stock.
The two sections of Figure 14.35 1 and 2 show the popup screen where you implement
the mass changes after choosing the Change button. Here, you select which fields you
want to changed and which fields should keep their existing values. For example, you
can manually define values in the Variability Indicator and Value Indicator dropdowns
because these can’t be calculated automatically if enough GIs haven’t been posted in
the past.
In this application, you can also define the Lead Time calculation methods for the Hori-
zon for Past (in Days) and Horizon for Future (in Days) settings. By default, the system
will calculate the lead time based on the values defined in the material master, but dif-
ferent methods are available in which the lead time will be calculated based on con-
firmed production orders. These fields will define the method and the number of days
the system will use to find confirmed production orders and calculate the lead time.
For this example, you can maintain a value of “15” in the Horizon for Future (in Days)
field 2 so that the system will consider the future planning horizon of 15 days with
demand, which is basically PIRs, during buffer level determination.
Figure 14.35 Mass Adjustments of Values of Products
Note
The ILT Calc. Method field (not shown but located further down screen 2 in Figure
14.35) is used to calculate the lead time. Lead time can be calculated based on its historic
average, which is the default method. Lead time, when predicted, is calculated using
© 

821
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
machine learning and will grow more accurate over time. Note that in the initial stages,
there might be a mismatch between the predictions and the actual lead times.
To maintain relevant configuration settings so that they appear in the ILT Calc. Method
field in Figure 14.35 2, you use the following configuration menu path in the SAP
S/4HANA system: SAP IMG  Production  Demand-Driven Replenishment  Define
Lead Time Calculation Method.

sections.title: Planning and Execution
sections.text:
14.5.6 Planning and Execution
Now, we’ll discuss the procedures that have been defined in SAP S/4HANA’s automated
planning to generate procurement proposals such as purchase requisitions and
planned orders. This is followed by interactive planning executed by the user to post-
process the results of the planning run. Here, there are two substeps involved. Priority
key figures support the decision to convert a procurement proposal (e.g., a planned
order) into a fixed element (a production order or a process order) or to convert a pur-
chase requisition into a purchase order. Finally, a production order is released for pro-
duction again on the basis of a priority key figure in a comparable step.
This means that the planning and execution procedure for DDR doesn’t differ from the
process flow of a classic MRP run. However, as already described in the previous sec-
tions, there is a significant difference in the control logic for the elements to be buff-
ered, and the planning and execution part of the DDR process is now also characterized
by specific features. We discuss the functions available for these three steps in the fol-
lowing two sections.
The following sections will also discuss how to use the decoupling points set previously
together with the buffer levels to generate appropriate procurement and in-house pro-
duction proposals. Furthermore, we’ll discuss three DDR-specific apps: Replenishment
Planning by Priority, Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status, and Planner Over-
view.
Automated Demand-Driven Replenishment Planning in the MRP Run
You can carry out the planning in DDR with SAP S/4HANA by using the normal MRP
run, which starts with running Transaction MD01N or using the Material Requirements
Planning app, for example.
Using the MRP type previously maintained, the planning run identifies the materials
for which the DDR logic is to be used. These must have planning procedure C indirectly
via the MRP type. The net flow equation is used for generating receipt elements:
On-hand stock + Open supply – Open past (including today’s) demand – Qualified spike
demand

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
822
In the planning run, the net flow position calculated as described is compared with the
TOY buffer level. If the net flow position is lower than TOY, then a receipt element is cre-
ated that raises the stock quantity to TOG. Figure 14.36 shows the results of a DDR plan-
ning run as an example.
Figure 14.36 Results of DDR Planning Run (Example)
The following input factors are used for the calculation:
 On-hand stock
The quantity available for the planning run that would also be used in other plan-
ning procedures in the MRP run
 Open supply
All receipts that have already been ordered but aren’t yet available
 Open demand (past, including current day):
– Dependent requirements
– Sales and distribution requirements (schedule lines, outbound deliveries, and
open sales order quantities)
– Stock transfer order requirements
– Stock transfer reservations (via movement type 541)
This explicitly excludes dependent requirements, PIRs, and stock transport requisi-
tions.
 Qualified spike demand
In particular, you should take high demand into account when determining the
qualified spike demand. This is a future daily requirement that lies above the spike
Red
Top of Red
(ToR) = 4.200
Units
Yellow
Top of Yellow
(ToY) = 8.000
Units
Green
Blue
Top of Green
(ToG) = 12.000
Units Created
Proposal =
12.000 – 7.350
= 4.650 Units
Net Flow
Position = 7.350
Units
© 

823
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
threshold within the spike horizon (see Figure 14.37). The spike threshold is defined
by a factor of the TOR, which is maintained at the plant level. The spike horizon is cal-
culated as follows:
Today + Factor × DLT + Constant offset
The input values for the factor and constant offset are also maintained at the plant level
and in the Buffer Profile app (see Figure 14.38).
Figure 14.37 Usage of Spikes in DDR Planning Run
Figure 14.38 Buffer Profile Settings at Plant Level (Plant 1710)
You apply forward scheduling by using the DLT, whereby the GR processing times and
the safety times of the horizon key are also included in the same way as for normal
MRP. We recommend that you eliminate these times to avoid multiple consideration.
To calm the planning results, you should use a planning time fence with firming type 1
(MRP Type D1 for DDR) so that a new element that has been moved by one day can’t be
created in the subsequent planning run.
Evaluating the DDR Results after MRP Run
Figure 14.39 and Figure 14.40 are part of the same Header Details screen, and they show
an example of the MRP results for Material 2269 in the Stock/Requirements List screen
(via Transaction MD04). On expanding the screen, notice the Demand-Driven Replen-
ishment tab and associated DDR information.
TOG
TOY
TOR
Time
Sales Order Quantity
Spike Horizon Today
Spike
Spike Threshold = 50% x TOR
(Past) Due SO’s
Demand, in particular Spikes

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
824
Figure 14.39 Stock/Requirements List
Figure 14.40 Stock/Requirements List: Detailed View
Let’s discuss and evaluate the DDR results for Material 2269 in Plant 1710. The mate-
rial’s Stock on Hand is 60, which is below the reorder point. The Open Supply quantity
is 100 and is basically a production order for which the goods aren’t delivered to the
warehouse yet. The Open Past Demand quantity (from the sales order) is 600, whereas
the Qualified Spike Dmd (the customer order for which the delivery date is within the
Spike Horizon Date of 07/22/2024) is 652.500.
The spike horizon is a time window that starts the day after today and comprises the
DLT multiplied by a factor called the spike horizon DLT multiplier, with the addition of
an offset called the spike horizon constant. The purpose here is that DDR will only react
to a demand within this time window to ensure product availability.
So, in this example, the Spike Horizon Constant is 5 (days), and the Spike Horizon DLT
Multiplier is 0.5. Referring to Figure 14.39 again, the DLT is 7 days. So, the calculation for
the spike horizon is as follows:
Spike horizon = 5 (Spike horizon constant) + (5 (DLT) × 0.5) = 7 days
This is shown in the Order Spike Horizon field in Figure 14.39.
© 

825
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
However, MRP won’t react to all the demand within the spike horizon—only to quali-
fied demands. To be considered a qualified demand, a requirement quantity must be
greater than the spike threshold, which is calculated by multiplying the safety stock by
the spike threshold factor. Only those highlighted demands will be considered quali-
fied demands because they are within the spike horizon and above the spike threshold.
The qualified spike demand of 600 is not considered by DDR because it’s smaller than
the Order Spike Threshold quantity of 652.500. An order spike threshold is a multiplica-
tion of the safety stock (in this example, 435.000) by the Spike Threshold (1.50) as
defined in the buffer profile (refer to Figure 14.38). Therefore, 435.000 × 1.5 = 652.500 is
the order spike threshold.
Note
The Spike Horizon Constant, Spike Horizon DLT Multiplier, Spike Threshold, and On-
Hand Alert Threshold settings are maintained at the plant level in the Buffer Profile
Maintenance app (see Figure 14.38 again).
However, there are additional scenarios in which MRP may generate a new replenish-
ment proposal for a DDR product even if the stock isn’t yet below the reorder point.
During the MRP calculation, the system will try to calculate the net flow position, using
the following equation:
On-hand stock + open supply – open past (including today’s) demand – qualified spike
demand
Referring to Figure 14.39 and this equation, the net flow position of 160 is calculated as
follows:
Net flow position: 60 + 100 = 160
Because the open past demand and any qualified spike demand are also considered in
the equation, the available quantity would be below the reorder point. Therefore, MRP
will create a new replenishment proposal to replenish up to the maximum stock level.
In the equation, the qualified spike demand, as well as Figure 14.40, shows that we have
a line with the End of Spike Horizon, so it’s important to define those terms for better
understanding of the MRP results for a demand-driven product.
Replenishment proposals created for a DDR product will always be forward scheduled,
considering today as the start date and using the DLT to calculate the finish date for
both externally and internally procured products. As shown earlier in Figure 14.39 and
Figure 14.40, in which the stock/requirements list for a demand-driven product is
shown, there’s also a planning time fence when using a demand-driven MRP type. This
time fence is always equal to the DLT, and it uses fixing type 1 by default. This time
fence exists to prevent the dates of a planned order created by MRP for a demand-
driven product from being changed in the following MRP run.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
826
The MRP run is usually executed daily as a background job, and it should ensure that
there are enough replenishment proposals to cover the DDR buffer. In addition to the
MRP run, a materials planner can use the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority
app and the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status app to monitor the buffer
coverage and take actions to avoid shortages, such as manually creating a new replen-
ishment proposal or expediting an existing order. These two apps are discussed in the
following sections.
Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority
You can postprocess the planning run by using the Replenishment Planning by Plan-
ning Priority app. After you call up the app, a buffer level overview will be displayed.
The planning priority app is used for sorting, which results in a percentage comparison
of TOG with the following value:
On-hand stock + Receipts – Relevant requirement elements
You can use the displayed results to convert planned orders into production orders and
process orders, or to convert purchase requisitions into purchase orders. The Replen-
ishment Planning by Planning Priority app displays a list of products within the user’s
AOR for which the net flow position is below the reorder point. However, you can
change this by using the filter option to add more planning priority statuses if you
need to see additional products. All those products will be sorted according to the plan-
ning priority, which is the percentage of the buffer covered by the net flow position.
The lines are highlighted in color according to the respective planning priority status.
Figure 14.41 shows an example in which the first three lines are highlighted in red
because the net flow position is below the safety stock and the fourth line is high-
lighted in blue because the net flow position is above the maximum stock level.
Figure 14.41 Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority App
© 

827
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
There are five different planning priority statuses:
 Above maximum stock
 At 0 or below with demand
 Below reorder point
 Below safety stock
 Below or at maximum stock
Figure 14.41 also shows an example of a planning run result sorted according to plan-
ning priority.
The screen shown in Figure 14.41 displays a Create Supply button on those lines for
which the net flow position is below the reorder point or the safety stock. If you choose
this option, DDR will create a new planned order to replenish this product up to the
maximum stock level. The scheduling logic for this planned order will be similar to the
logic used by MRP: it uses forward scheduling, considering today as the start date and
using the DLT to calculate the finish date.
Next, select a specific line in Figure 14.41 and then choose the forward arrow located on
the far-right side of the screen. This brings up the Supply/Demand List tab and addi-
tional information about the product, as shown in Figure 14.42.
Figure 14.42 Supply/Demand List
The Supply/Demand List tab consists of information about the existing demand and
replenishment elements. You can edit or delete an existing replenishment element or
manually create a new planned order by defining the quantity, date, and source of supply.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
828
The Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app shown in Figure 14.42 focuses on
the planning side because it’s based on the comparison of the net flow position with
the reorder point. Meanwhile, the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status app
(see Figure 14.43), discussed next, is used to manage short-term execution. It focuses on
the comparison of the on-hand stock with the safety stock.
Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status
Replenishment execution according to the DDR logic is performed with the Replenish-
ment Execution by On-Hand Status app. In this app, the current warehouse stock situ-
ation is displayed, and the on-hand status is used for prioritization, which is calculated
as follows:
On-hand stock (without receipt and requirement elements) compared to TOR (in %)
This app will show, by default, a list of products for which the on-hand stock is below
the safety stock (the red zone), but you can include additional products by changing the
default filters. Figure 14.43 shows this list of products, where the icons in the On-Hand
Buffer Status column highlight products with stock below the safety stock.
Whenever there is an open replenishment proposal for a product for which the stock is
below the safety stock, the Expedite Supply button will appear in the Execute Action
column. By choosing this option, you can change the dates of an existing replenish-
ment element to ensure that the safety stock is covered as quickly as possible, thus
avoiding any potential product shortage.
Figure 14.43 Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status App
© 

829
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
Note
By default, the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app will only show the
option to create a new supply, and the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status
app will only show the option to expedite an existing supply. However, you can change
the column settings (via the filter option) in both applications to include a column for
the missing action. You can also include additional columns with important informa-
tion, such as the reorder point or the safety stock.
When you choose the option to Expedite Supply in Figure 14.43, a popup appears (see
Figure 14.44) that displays a list of the existing replenishment proposals and additional
information, such as the source, status, and order progress (in case of production
orders). You can also change the date in the Planned Availability Date column to expe-
dite the replenishment proposal.
Figure 14.44 Expedite Supply
Alternatively, instead of choosing the option to expedite a replenishment proposal as
shown in this example, you can simply choose one of the lines to see the Supply/
Demand List and additional information about the demand-driven product, exactly as
in the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app shown earlier in Figure 14.41.
You as a materials planner can also use the Projected On-Hand Alerts app (see Figure
14.45) to identify situations in which the projected stock within the decoupled lead time
will not be sufficient to cover all the requirements. The following are the alerts to
choose from:
 Stockout Alert
This alert appears when the requirement will lead to a shortage within the decou-
pled lead time.
 Safety Stock Alert
This alert appears when the requirement causes the projected stock to fall below the
safety stock within the decoupled lead time.

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
830
 Threshold Alert
This alert appears when the projected stock falls below the safety stock threshold as
defined in configuration.
You can navigate to the detailed screen not only to get more information about the var-
ious alerts but also to create new replenishment proposals.
Figure 14.45 Projected On-Hand Alerts
Planner Overview
The Planner Overview app displays an overview of all materials that need to be treated
manually. Therefore, it considers one or both of the following situations:
 Buffer level calculation results need attention or action.
 Planning status and on-hand status need attention or action.
There are three different tiles in this application, as shown in Figure 14.46:
 Buffer Level Management
This tile shows the products with deviating buffers that a planner should approve.
 Replenishment Planning
Similar to the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app, this tile shows
products with a net flow position below the reorder point.
 Replenishment Execution
Similar to the Replenishment Execution by Planning Priority app, this tile shows
products with a stock level below the safety stock level.
The Planner Overview app enables you to choose the breakdown by procurement type
or by each DDR product classification (ABC, EFG, PQR, or XYZ). You do this by choosing
the relevant option from the dropdown available in all three tiles. Within this app, you
can also choose any of the bars in the charts to drill down into the application and see
© 

831
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
the respective list of products. For example, in Figure 14.46, there is one product with
the Buy procurement type on the chart for the Replenishment Planning tile. Choosing
this bar will branch you into the Replenishment Planning app, which will display this
specific product. Therefore, you can use this application as a central point of entry from
which you can monitor the main DDR KPIs and take actions to correct problems and
deviations.
Figure 14.46 Planner Overview
Historical Buffer Performance
The Historical Buffer Performance app (see Figure 14.47) is a new app that shows the his-
torical data of a material buffer stock within a specified time period.
Figure 14.47 Historical Buffer Performance
The app compares the material stock with the buffer in the previous month and shows
the number of days a material was outside the outlier area (red zones). You can change
the Evaluation Horizon to different time period or granularity—such as last week, last

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
832
month, last quarter, or even a custom period as long as it is within a maximum of 92
days. If you need to, you can even drill down into the historical buffer position to every
single day of the week to see how that view compares with the actual stock situation.
The next section will discuss important KPIs and metrics relevant to DDR, including
inventory position and inventory sizing. Furthermore, it will discuss the factors that
affect the DDR process.

sections.title: Metrics and Analytics
sections.text:
14.5.7 Metrics and Analytics
As discussed in Section 14.2, the key to ensuring success in materials planning and a
smoother supply chain using the DDR approach is to focus on protecting and promot-
ing relevant material and information flows. Doing so is only possible by setting up
metrics (KPIs) and leveraging the power of analytics to bring constant improvements.
The three factors responsible for smoother and more efficient demand-driven plan-
ning are demand signal reliability, stability, and velocity, and we’ll cover those later in
this section. Let’s first discuss the key metrics of DDR.
Key Metrics
DDMRP requires setting up inventory buffers at strategic points in the supply chain to
independently plan, schedule, and execute material supply based on actual demand.
This is a practice known as inventory positioning. Not only is the inventory position
critical, but so is determining how much inventory to place to bring harmony between
material demand and supply. This is a practice known as inventory sizing. Effective
inventory positioning and inventory sizing immediately lead to business benefits
(such as reduction in working capital, storage costs, and material expediting costs)
while increasing production efficiencies through effective machine capacity and
resource utilization. These key metrics are discussed in the following sections.
Inventory Positioning
A standard graphical representation of inventory positioning will show the on-hand
stock position of a buffered material wherein the x-axis is the time range, and the y-axis
is the stock position. The black dotted line reflects the time series of material
replenishment. It’s important to note the instances when this buffered material pene-
trated deep into the alert zones, meaning the yellow zone and even the red zone. Evalu-
ating such instances by addressing the root causes that led to these zone penetrations
often reveals the prime areas for improvement. Some of the root causes for such situa-
tions can be the fact that the materials planner didn’t place a timely replenishment
order (i.e., waited too long), an shipment from a supplier was inordinately delayed, or
there was a production breakdown. Similarly, companies often evaluate a supplier’s per-
formance based on the number of times there were stock-outs due to that particular
supplier’s delayed material delivery, how long such delays lasted, and how many parent
© 

833
14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
materials were affected. Along with analytics, these key supplier metrics (KPIs) help the
company make informed decisions on whether to continue with the same supplier,
work on an improvement plan, or simply look for alternative sources of supply.
Figure 14.48 is another view of key metrics for stock buffers (on the left side of the fig-
ure) and capacity buffers (on the right side). Again, the dashed lines in both figures are
situations worth evaluating for improvement for understock, overstock, and capacity.
To provide a more holistic view of such alert zones, such as dark red, red, and yellow, it
often makes sense to evaluate them over a longer-term horizon rather than over a
shorter one. Doing so will ensure that the analytics can depict a broader trend over a
relatively longer period of time instead of showing a sudden erosion of zones due to a
one-time supplier delay or an unforeseen machine breakdown.
Figure 14.48 Focusing on Outliers (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission)
Inventory Sizing
Similarly, evaluating a group of materials with similar characteristics (e.g., materials
with long lead times, several materials ordered from the same supplier or a group of
suppliers, and production materials facing delays due to bottleneck capacities at spe-
cific work centers) provides greater insights for improvement initiatives than evaluat-
ing individual materials’ replenishment situations at decoupling points.
Here’s an example of typical stages or phases wherein you can use analytics to monitor
the replenishment’s critical metrics and then undertake improvement initiatives to
monitor the progress of these critical metrics. Stage 1 shows a bimodal distribution of
material stock wherein there were many deep penetrations into the yellow, red, and
dark red zones for both stock-outs and overstock situations. This alarming situation is
reflected by the dashed lines at both ends of the two curves. At stage 2, the DDR’s imple-
mentation takes place, and the situation improves significantly. However, notice that
there are still outliers on both sides of the curve, as shown by the dashed lines. There’s
still some room for improvement at both ends, by eliminating the stock-out and over-
stock situations. Additional buffer improvement initiatives, such as procuring materials
Dark Red Dark Red
0
Optimal Range
Warning
Y R

Y R

Optimal

Warning (Low)
Extreme High
Extreme Low

14 Demand-Driven Replenishment
834
from alternative sources that may be slightly more expensive but can greatly reduce the
replenishment lead time, finally result in stage 3.
Factors Affecting Demand-Driven Replenishment
Finally, let’s discuss the three factors that play a critical role in measuring demand-driven
metrics and providing reliable analytics:
 Signal integrity
Signal integrity in the supply chain monitors whether there’s complete, correct, and
timely communication of the required data and relevant information to ensure
timely replenishment. In other words, signal integrity status is a measure of how
rapidly SAP S/4HANA and the planners respond to the demand signals generated by
SAP S/4HANA. This assessment is made by comparing each day’s net flow position
against TOG, which is for order recommendation and not order execution.
This replenishment should not only be timely but also based on the recommended
quantity suggested by the DDR approach. A delayed placement of a replenishment
order or a quantity mismatch will definitely lead to buffer erosion that will lead you
into one of the alert zones.
 Decoupling point integrity
A decoupling point is an independent stock position in the supply chain that is strate-
gically positioned to absorb the effects of demand variability or supply disruptions—
factors that are all too common in today’s volatile and complex supply chain. To
ensure the integrity and reliability of a decoupling point, it’s important to constantly
evaluate the reasons for, factors behind, and causes of deviations in relevant material
and information flows. Using analytics, you can determine the materials that exhibit
a large variability between planned and actual supply order management and make
them immediate candidates for attention and corrective action. With analytics, you
can compare the on-hand inventory value with TOY (the execution zone) to generate
a status expressed as a percentage.
 Decoupling point velocity
The velocity at a decoupling point measures the frequency of planned and actual
placement of supply orders. This measurement is known as order frequency vari-
ance. For example, say that after you implement DDR planning, 10 purchase orders
for a material are planned to be placed with a vendor on a monthly basis. However,
in actuality, 5,616 purchase orders were placed within the same time period. This
deviation between planned and actual purchase orders points to an area where
order frequency needs to increase. Similarly, a group of materials belonging to the
same classifications or attributes are measured for flow index, meaning how their
ordering frequencies compare with their ADU. In addition, although the focus is
often on materials that move too slowly or are ordered too infrequently, an equally
important factor to look into is those materials that are ordered very frequently.
They mask the actual velocity of materials’ movements and therefore are candidates
© 

835

sections.title: Summary
sections.text:
14.6 Summary
for improvement in materials flow. With analytics, you can monitor such critical
material flow metrics and take action to improve the flow index.
14.6 Summary
DDR shifts the entire paradigm from the “supply” side of planning to the “demand”
side, wherein only the materials in demand are procured and produced to reduce stock-
outs and overstock situations. This chapter covered the necessary DDR configuration
and master data setup, and it then reviewed a few relevant transactions to accurately
and correctly reflect them in the DDR business processes. The DDR business processes
consist of classifying products, positioning buffers, and calculating buffers. We also dis-
cussed the planning and execution steps of DDR.
The next chapter covers predictive material requirements planning (pMRP), which is
an innovative materials and capacity planning simulation tool that can help you
improve your logistics and supply chain management.

©
